question: What first drew you to Philosophy, and what keeps you interested in it?
lookingFor: A genuine origin story tied to a specific question or moment, showing sustained curiosity rather than a vague pull toward 'big questions'.

bullets: Name a specific question, text, argument, or experience that sparked the interest
Show the interest has deepened over time — not just a first impression
Connect it to what you want from a degree, not just what you already know
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It started when I was about fifteen and got into an argument with my physics teacher about whether the laws of nature are discovered or invented. I couldn't let it go, and when I found out there was a whole field built around exactly that kind of question I felt like I'd been handed a key. Since then I've read more widely — Hume on causation, Kuhn on scientific revolutions — and the more I read, the more I realise the questions get harder rather than easier, which is actually what draws me. I want a degree that teaches me to argue more carefully, not one that just gives me more answers to memorise.

commonMistakes: Citing 'big existential questions' with no specific example of one you've actually wrestled with
Treating Philosophy as a fallback because it doesn't require maths or lab work
Describing interest in a single sub-field (e.g. ethics) as if it's the whole discipline
followUps: What's the question in Philosophy that you find most difficult to stop thinking about?

question: Why do you want to study Philosophy at degree level rather than just reading about it independently?
lookingFor: An understanding of what a formal degree adds — rigour, method, dialogue, structured argument — over autodidactic reading.

bullets: Acknowledge what independent reading has already given you
Identify what a degree provides that solo reading can't — structured argument, seminar challenge, supervision
Show awareness that philosophical thinking is a discipline, not just an opinion
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've read quite a lot on my own and found it genuinely rewarding, but I've also realised I have a habit of accepting arguments I find elegant without really stress-testing them. When I did a philosophy taster session, the tutor took apart a claim I thought was airtight in about thirty seconds, and that moment was humbling and exciting. A degree gives me people who will do that systematically, and the technical vocabulary to do it myself — I want to learn to argue well, not just to think interesting thoughts.

commonMistakes: Implying the degree is mostly just 'more reading', missing the analytical training
Not being able to say what rigorous philosophical method actually involves
Sounding like the degree is a credential rather than a genuine intellectual project
followUps: What's a philosophical argument you've read recently that you found genuinely convincing?

question: Why do you want to study Philosophy here specifically?
lookingFor: Specific, researched reasons tied to faculty, modules, or the department's strengths — not generic praise.

bullets: Name something specific: a module, a professor's research area, a department feature
Connect it to a genuine intellectual interest of yours
Show you understand how this course is structured differently from others
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn in by the philosophy of mind options in your second year — specifically the module on consciousness and personal identity, which sits at the intersection of metaphysics and cognitive science, and that's exactly where my reading has been taking me. I also read a paper by one of your faculty on the extended mind thesis and found the argument genuinely surprising, which made me want to study somewhere that takes that kind of cross-disciplinary work seriously. The joint-honours pathway with psychology is a route I'd seriously consider, and I don't think many departments let you take that combination.

commonMistakes: Citing only rankings or vague reputation
Praising modules that don't actually exist at this department
Making the answer about the city or campus rather than the intellectual environment
followUps: What would you contribute to philosophical discussion here?

question: Tell us about a philosophical book, essay, or argument you've engaged with recently. What did you make of it?
lookingFor: Critical engagement with a real text — able to summarise an argument, identify its strengths, and push back or extend it.

bullets: Briefly state the core argument — show you actually understood it
Offer a genuine response: what convinced you, what didn't, and why
Make it a conversation, not a book report
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Nagel's 'What Is It Like to Be a Bat?' last term and found it genuinely unsettling in a productive way. The argument is that subjective experience has a character — the 'what it's likeness' — that physical descriptions can't capture, even in principle. I find that persuasive as a diagnosis of the hard problem, but I'm less sure Nagel is right that we can't imagine bat experience at all — I think he might be conflating imagining from the outside with imagining from the inside. That distinction felt important and I wasn't sure the essay resolved it.

commonMistakes: Summarising the text without offering any view of your own
Naming a very famous popular-philosophy book (like Sophie's World) without engaging with actual arguments
Pretending to have read something you haven't — panels can probe very quickly
followUps: What would you say to someone who disagreed with Nagel entirely and thought consciousness is fully physical?

question: Do you think human beings have free will? How would you defend your view?
lookingFor: Awareness of the main positions (compatibilism, hard determinism, libertarianism) and the ability to reason through a position rather than just assert one.

bullets: Distinguish between the key positions rather than treating it as a simple yes/no
Pick a view and defend it with at least one argument
Acknowledge the strongest objection to your view
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm drawn to compatibilism — the idea that free will and determinism are compatible if we define free will as acting according to your own desires and reasons, free from compulsion, rather than requiring some gap in the causal chain. I find the hard determinist position hard to live with: when I deliberate about what to do, that deliberation feels like it genuinely matters, and compatibilism lets me say it does. The strongest objection I can see is that if my desires are themselves caused by prior events, compatibilism just pushes the problem back. I don't think that fully undermines it, but I hold the view with some uncertainty.

commonMistakes: Treating the question as purely a matter of personal belief without engaging philosophical positions
Ignoring determinism entirely when arguing for free will
Picking a position and refusing to engage with objections
followUps: Does your answer change if neuroscience shows all decisions are predictable in advance?

question: Can you walk us through what makes a philosophical argument valid versus merely persuasive?
lookingFor: Understanding of logical validity vs. soundness vs. rhetorical persuasion — shows analytical training, not just opinion-sharing.

bullets: Distinguish validity (structure) from soundness (structure + true premises)
Note that persuasive arguments can be neither valid nor sound — rhetoric, emotional appeal, familiarity
Give a simple example to anchor the distinction
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A valid argument is one where, if the premises were true, the conclusion would have to be true — it's about structure. A sound argument is valid and also has premises that are actually true. An argument can be persuasive without being either: a speaker might use vivid language or social pressure or confirmation bias to make a conclusion feel obvious, even if the reasoning is broken. A classic example is affirming the consequent — 'if it rains the ground is wet; the ground is wet; therefore it rained' — which feels reasonable but is formally invalid. Philosophy is largely the project of noticing which persuasive arguments are actually sound and which aren't.

commonMistakes: Using 'valid' to mean 'good' or 'convincing' in the everyday sense
Not being able to give any example of an invalid but persuasive argument
Treating soundness as purely a matter of logical form
followUps: Can you give an example of a valid argument with a false premise?

question: The trolley problem asks whether you'd divert a runaway trolley to kill one person instead of five. What does your answer reveal about your ethical commitments?
lookingFor: Ability to use the thought experiment as a tool for exposing moral intuitions, not just give the popular answer — shows the student understands what thought experiments are for.

bullets: Give your intuitive response and then immediately interrogate it
Connect it to the consequentialist vs. deontological distinction
Show awareness that the case is designed to pull intuitions apart, not resolve ethics
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My instinct is to pull the lever — five lives outweigh one feels like obvious arithmetic. But the reason the thought experiment is useful is that it reveals a consequentialist pull in my intuition, and then you can ask whether I'm comfortable with everything consequentialism implies: would I push a large person off a bridge to stop the trolley? Most people including me say no, and that's the interesting gap. I think thought experiments like this are valuable less for giving ethical answers and more for mapping which principles we actually hold and where they conflict — which is most of the interesting work in moral philosophy.

commonMistakes: Treating the answer to the trolley problem itself as the point
Not knowing the difference between the lever and footbridge versions
Refusing to engage with the hypothetical because it's unrealistic
followUps: What does the footbridge variant (pushing someone off a bridge) reveal that the lever version doesn't?

question: How do you know that the external world is real and not a dream or a simulation?
lookingFor: Engagement with Cartesian or simulation scepticism and awareness of the main responses — not a dismissal of the question.

bullets: Take the sceptical challenge seriously rather than dismissing it as absurd
Sketch at least one serious response: pragmatic, Moorean, coherentist, or contextualist
Show awareness that the debate hasn't been definitively settled
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Strictly speaking I can't prove it — any evidence I might cite could in principle be part of the dream or simulation. Descartes showed that pretty convincingly. I find Moore's response interesting: he held up his hands and said 'here is one hand, here is another' — not as proof, but as a reminder that the certainty I have about my hands is more reliable than any philosophical premise that could undermine it. I'm sympathetic to that, though I don't think it fully resolves the epistemological question. What I take from it is that sceptical scenarios set the bar for knowledge so high that almost nothing counts, which maybe tells us something about the concept of knowledge itself rather than about the world.

commonMistakes: Simply asserting 'of course the world is real' without engaging the argument
Not knowing Descartes' argument well enough to reconstruct it
Claiming the simulation hypothesis is testable and therefore not a philosophical problem
followUps: Does it matter practically whether we can prove the external world is real?

question: What does it mean for a word to have meaning? Is meaning in the word, in the speaker's head, or somewhere else?
lookingFor: Awareness of the major theories of meaning — ideational, referential, use — and ability to push past naive answers.

bullets: Identify the naive picture (meaning = mental image) and show why it's insufficient
Introduce at least one alternative: Fregean sense/reference, Wittgenstein on use, or causal-historical theory
Show genuine uncertainty rather than false confidence
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The obvious answer is that meaning is a mental image — 'dog' means the concept of a dog in my head. But Wittgenstein points out that this can't be the whole story: if I have an image of a brown dog, is it the meaning of 'dog' or 'brown dog'? The image doesn't carry its own rules for application. His alternative — that meaning is use, that words mean what we do with them in language games — feels more robust to me, though it raises its own puzzles about how new uses get established. Frege's distinction between sense and reference is also useful: 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' pick out the same object but mean something different, which suggests meaning can't just be what a word refers to. I don't think there's a settled answer, and that's part of what makes the philosophy of language genuinely hard.

commonMistakes: Stopping at the naive mental-image theory without challenging it
Confusing reference (what a word picks out) with meaning (how it picks it out)
Treating the question as having an obvious common-sense answer
followUps: What does your view imply for whether two people who use the word 'red' mean the same thing?

question: If your brain were transplanted into another body, where would you be — in the new body, or the old one?
lookingFor: Engagement with psychological continuity vs. bodily continuity theories of personal identity, using the case as a diagnostic tool.

bullets: Articulate why the case is philosophically interesting — it forces a choice between competing criteria
Identify the two main views: psychological continuity (Locke/Parfit) vs. bodily/animalist view
Give a view and notice what it commits you to
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My instinct is that I'd be in the new body, because I tend to think personal identity is about continuity of memory, personality, and psychological life — the brain carries those, so wherever the brain goes, I go. That's the Lockean view, roughly. But it has odd implications: if my brain were split and each half implanted in a different body, where am I then? Parfit argues this shows that identity isn't what we should care about — what matters is psychological continuity, and that can come in degrees or branch. I find that quite counterintuitive but hard to argue against cleanly. The animalist would say I go with the body, because I'm a biological organism, not just a collection of mental states — and that view is harder to dismiss than it sounds.

commonMistakes: Treating the question as obvious without exploring what it reveals about personal identity criteria
Not knowing any of the major positions by name or argument
Refusing to commit to any view at all
followUps: Does Parfit's branching case worry you, and what would you say about it?

question: Is morality objective, or does it vary between cultures and individuals? How do you reason about that?
lookingFor: Ability to distinguish descriptive from normative relativism and engage metaethical positions without collapsing into either naive realism or easy relativism.

bullets: Distinguish descriptive relativism (cultures differ) from normative relativism (therefore all views are equally valid)
Identify why the inference from one to the other is problematic
Give a view on metaethics — realist, constructivist, or expressivist — and defend it briefly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The fact that cultures disagree about morality is undeniable, but I don't think it follows that morality is therefore just a matter of cultural opinion — cultures also disagree about the age of the universe, and that doesn't make cosmology subjective. The interesting question is whether there are moral facts independent of what anyone believes. I lean towards a constructivist picture: moral truths aren't written into the fabric of the universe, but they're not arbitrary either — they're what rational agents would agree to under the right conditions. That lets me say cultural practices like slavery are genuinely wrong, not just wrong 'by my standards', which I think any metaethics needs to be able to do.

commonMistakes: Equating cultural diversity in moral practice with moral relativism being correct
Treating 'objective' as meaning 'obvious' or 'agreed upon'
Refusing to take a view because 'everyone has their own opinion'
followUps: How would a moral realist respond to the argument from disagreement?

question: What is the mind-body problem, and do you think there is a satisfying solution to it?
lookingFor: Clear grasp of the hard problem of consciousness and awareness of the main positions — physicalism, dualism, functionalism — with genuine critical evaluation.

bullets: State the problem clearly: how does physical brain activity give rise to subjective experience
Distinguish the hard problem from the easy problems (function vs. phenomenal consciousness)
Give a considered view on whether physicalism, dualism, or another position handles it best
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The mind-body problem, at its sharpest, is Chalmers' hard problem: even if we fully explained every cognitive function — attention, memory, discrimination — there would still be a further question of why any of it is accompanied by subjective experience, by something it's like to be us. Physicalism says consciousness is identical to or reducible to brain states, which handles the functional side well, but I find the explanatory gap genuinely worrying — it's not obvious how neural firing causes the redness of red. Dualism avoids that but creates the interaction problem: how does a non-physical mind affect a physical body? I find myself most sympathetic to a non-reductive physicalism or perhaps illusionism — the idea that phenomenal consciousness as we conceive it might itself be a kind of cognitive error — though I hold that tentatively because it's uncomfortable.

commonMistakes: Treating the mind-body problem as simply 'does the brain create thought' without the consciousness angle
Not knowing Chalmers' distinction between hard and easy problems
Asserting physicalism or dualism without acknowledging the difficulties each faces
followUps: What do you think illusionism — the view that phenomenal consciousness is itself an illusion — gets right or wrong?

question: Rawls argued that principles of justice should be chosen behind a 'veil of ignorance', not knowing your place in society. Do you find that a compelling framework?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with Rawls' methodology and the difference principle, plus awareness of standard objections from libertarians or communitarians.

bullets: Explain the veil of ignorance and what work it does — stripping out self-interest to reach impartial principles
Evaluate what it delivers: the difference principle, basic liberties
Raise at least one serious objection — Nozick's entitlement theory, or the communitarian critique of the 'unencumbered self'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: What I find compelling about the veil of ignorance is the thought behind it: if you don't know whether you'll be born rich or poor, talented or not, you have a reason to care about the worst-off, because you might be them. That generates the difference principle — inequalities are only justified if they benefit the least advantaged — which I think is more defensible than pure equality or pure desert. The objection I take most seriously is Nozick's: that if holdings are justly acquired through voluntary exchange, redistributing them is a violation of rights regardless of what the veil of ignorance would choose. I'm not sure Nozick is right, but the tension between procedural justice and patterned outcomes feels genuinely unresolved.

commonMistakes: Confusing the veil of ignorance with ignorance in general rather than its specific role as a thought experiment
Not being able to state the difference principle
Treating Rawls as obviously right or obviously wrong without engaging the tension
followUps: How would a libertarian like Nozick respond to the veil of ignorance methodology?

question: Which topics from your A-level or pre-university study have most shaped how you think philosophically?
lookingFor: Evidence that curriculum work has been processed critically, not just memorised — shows independent intellectual engagement.

bullets: Name specific topics or thinkers from the syllabus and say what genuinely engaged you
Show how studying them changed or developed your thinking
Identify where the syllabus left you wanting to go further
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The epistemology module was the one that stayed with me — particularly Descartes and then Hume's response to the problem of induction. I found it unsettling that Hume seems to be right: there's no logically valid inference from past regularities to future ones. The textbook answer was Popper's falsificationism, but even that felt like it was changing the subject rather than answering Hume. That led me to read more about the problem independently, and I ended up in Bayesian epistemology, which I found genuinely more satisfying. I want a degree that takes me into that kind of depth, rather than treating the textbook answers as settled.

commonMistakes: Listing all the topics on the syllabus without distinguishing what personally engaged you
Showing no extension beyond what was taught — no follow-up reading or questions
Treating A-level philosophy as adequate preparation without hunger for more
followUps: What's the question from your A-level work that you most want to revisit at university level?

question: Karl Popper argued that a theory is scientific only if it's falsifiable. Is that a convincing criterion?
lookingFor: Understanding of falsificationism as a demarcation criterion, awareness of its problems, and engagement with alternatives.

bullets: State falsificationism clearly: a theory must in principle be shown false by observation
Identify what it does well — distinguishing science from astrology or Freudianism
Raise a genuine problem — the Duhem-Quine thesis, or theories like string theory that resist falsification
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Falsificationism is appealing because it gives a crisp answer to why astrology isn't science — a good astrologer can explain any outcome after the fact, while a falsifiable theory commits itself to specific predictions that could fail. But the criterion has real problems. The Duhem-Quine thesis points out that when an experiment contradicts a prediction, it's never just the theory that's being tested — it's the theory plus all the auxiliary assumptions, and you can always save the theory by adjusting the assumptions. Einstein's general relativity could have been 'saved' from the 1919 eclipse results with a fudge factor. Also, some physics that we take very seriously — string theory, eternal inflation — makes predictions we can't currently test. So falsifiability seems too blunt as a definition of science, even if it captures something real about scientific reasoning.

commonMistakes: Treating falsificationism as obviously correct because Popper is well known
Not being able to state what the Duhem-Quine problem is
Concluding that because Popper has problems, demarcation is hopeless
followUps: What would you put in place of falsificationism as a criterion for good science?

question: What is the purpose of thought experiments in philosophy? Can they prove anything?
lookingFor: An appreciation of thought experiments as probes of intuition and conceptual structure, with awareness of their limits.

bullets: Articulate what thought experiments do: test intuitions, reveal conceptual commitments, pump philosophical intuition
Give an example of a famous one and what it exposed
Acknowledge the limits: intuitions can be unreliable, cases can be too remote
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Thought experiments are tools for uncovering what we're actually committed to when we use concepts like 'knowledge' or 'identity' — they're not proofs in the mathematical sense, but they're not merely illustrative either. Gettier's cases, for instance, showed that justified true belief isn't sufficient for knowledge by constructing a scenario where all three conditions hold but we instinctively say someone doesn't really know — that single paragraph reshaped epistemology for decades. The limitation is that our intuitions can be culturally specific or cognitively biased, so a thought experiment reveals what we think, not necessarily what's true. I think the right response is to treat strong intuitions as evidence that needs explaining, not as verdicts.

commonMistakes: Treating thought experiments as just hypothetical stories rather than philosophical tools
Saying thought experiments can't prove anything and therefore aren't useful
Not being able to name or describe any actual thought experiment
followUps: Is there a thought experiment you think philosophers rely on too much or that has been over-interpreted?

question: Pick an applied ethical issue you've thought about. Walk us through how you reason about it.
lookingFor: Ability to apply ethical frameworks to a real issue, showing both rigour and awareness that reasonable people disagree.

bullets: Choose an issue you genuinely care about and have thought about carefully
Apply at least two different ethical frameworks and show what each yields
Acknowledge the limits of your view and the strongest counter-argument
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've thought a lot about whether it's ethical to eat animals, partly because it's a question I face personally. From a utilitarian perspective the case against it is quite strong: the suffering involved in factory farming is vast, and the pleasure humans get from the food seems not to outweigh it. A Kantian approach is more ambiguous — Kant himself didn't extend moral status to animals, but his principle of treating rational beings as ends gets complicated by the question of which animals have the relevant kind of rational agency. I've moved toward thinking the utilitarian concern is hard to dismiss, though I notice my intuitions are heavily influenced by what I've grown up with, which makes me less certain. What I try to do is at least reason consistently — which is why I find moral reasoning valuable even when it doesn't resolve everything.

commonMistakes: Picking an issue and then giving only personal opinion without ethical frameworks
Showing no awareness that the issue is genuinely contested
Choosing an issue too sensitive to engage with critically in the interview
followUps: How would a virtue ethicist approach the same issue differently?

question: Are you drawn more to analytic or continental philosophy, and do you think the distinction matters?
lookingFor: Awareness of what distinguishes the two traditions — method, style, concerns — and a genuine view on whether the division is productive or artificial.

bullets: Show you understand the distinction in terms of method and emphasis, not just nationality
Give a genuine view on which approach resonates more and why
Engage with whether the distinction is productive or a barrier
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My reading has been mostly analytic — I'm drawn to the precision of the arguments and the tendency to break questions into smaller, testable pieces. But I've also read some Heidegger and found that the way he frames the question of Being captures something that a more technical approach can miss — a sense of the strangeness of existence that gets dissolved by the very precision meant to illuminate it. I think the distinction is partly methodological and partly sociological, and I worry it functions as a boundary that stops philosophers reading work that would actually help them. Personally I'm more comfortable with analytic method, but I want to understand what the continental tradition is doing rather than just dismissing it as obscure.

commonMistakes: Identifying the split as purely geographical (British/American vs. French/German)
Dismissing continental philosophy without having read any
Not being able to characterise either tradition's method
followUps: What do you think analytic philosophy most consistently undervalues?

question: How has studying or being interested in other subjects — science, history, literature — shaped how you approach philosophical questions?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine interdisciplinary thinking and ability to draw philosophical problems out of non-philosophical material.

bullets: Name a specific subject and a specific connection to a philosophical question
Show how the other discipline raised the question, not just illustrated it
Avoid treating philosophy as superior to or merely explaining other disciplines
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My interest in physics raised the most philosophical questions for me. When I studied quantum mechanics and got to the measurement problem — the fact that the formalism doesn't tell you what happens when you observe a particle — I realised this was a philosophy of physics question, not just a technical loose end. Different interpretations (Copenhagen, Many Worlds, Bohmian mechanics) make the same empirical predictions but have radically different metaphysical pictures. That taught me that even the most rigorous empirical science doesn't settle every question about what the world is like — and that philosophers of physics are doing something genuinely important, not just tidying up.

commonMistakes: Treating the connection as purely illustrative ('physics gives examples for philosophy')
Picking an obvious link (literature → narrative, history → causation) without depth
Implying that other disciplines are just applied philosophy
followUps: What's a question from that other field that you think philosophy hasn't yet answered well?

question: Which philosopher has influenced your thinking most, and what exactly about their work do you find valuable?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine engagement with a specific philosopher's arguments — not just name-dropping — and critical appreciation rather than uncritical admiration.

bullets: Name a philosopher and a specific argument or text, not just a general reputation
Explain what you find genuinely valuable and what you find limited or problematic
Show the influence on how you actually think, not just what you've read
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Hume is the one I keep returning to. What I find most valuable is his combination of rigour and intellectual honesty — he follows the argument wherever it goes, even when it leads somewhere uncomfortable, like his conclusion that we have no rational basis for believing the sun will rise tomorrow. What I also appreciate is that he seems genuinely surprised by his own conclusions rather than performing scepticism. Where I find him limited is in his account of personal identity: the bundle theory — that the self is just a collection of perceptions — feels like it dissolves the question rather than answering it. But even that limitation is interesting to think with rather than against.

commonMistakes: Naming a philosopher whose work you can't actually describe in argument form
Treating your chosen philosopher as beyond criticism
Picking someone because they sound impressive rather than because you've genuinely engaged with them
followUps: What's the strongest objection you'd raise against this philosopher's central argument?

question: What is a logical fallacy, and can you give an example of one you've encountered in everyday life?
lookingFor: Understanding that fallacies are specific identifiable errors in reasoning, not just 'bad arguments', plus a real-world example showing the skill is lived not just studied.

bullets: Define a fallacy precisely: a form of argument that appears valid but contains a structural error
Name and explain a specific fallacy, not just say 'that doesn't follow'
Give a genuine everyday example rather than a textbook one
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A logical fallacy is a type of argument that looks reasonable but has a structural flaw that makes the conclusion fail to follow from the premises. The one I notice most in everyday life is the ad hominem — attacking the person making an argument rather than the argument itself. A few months ago someone dismissed a policy proposal by pointing out that the person proposing it had changed their view years ago, as if inconsistency in the past settles whether the current argument is sound. The policy itself was never engaged with. I find spotting fallacies is more useful as a listening habit than a debating tactic — it slows me down and makes me check whether I actually have a counter-argument or just a counter-feeling.

commonMistakes: Defining fallacy vaguely as 'a mistake in an argument' without noting the structural dimension
Giving only textbook examples with no personal instance
Confusing informal fallacies with formal logical invalidity
followUps: Is there a fallacy you catch yourself committing?

question: Is beauty objective or in the eye of the beholder? How would you defend your answer philosophically?
lookingFor: Engagement with the debate between aesthetic realism and subjectivism, with awareness of Hume's and Kant's positions and the distinction between taste and judgement.

bullets: Distinguish between pure subjectivism and the more nuanced positions (Hume's standard of taste, Kant on universal subjective judgements)
Note what's right and wrong about 'beauty is in the eye of the beholder'
Give a view and defend it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Pure subjectivism — beauty is just whatever I happen to like — struggles to explain why we bother arguing about taste, why some people's aesthetic judgements command more authority than others', or why our own sense of beauty develops over time. Hume thought there was a standard of taste grounded in ideal critical conditions — a practised observer with experience, good sense, and no prejudice — which isn't purely subjective. Kant took a different angle: aesthetic judgements claim universal assent ('this is beautiful' not 'I like this') but are grounded in subjective feeling rather than concepts, which makes them neither purely objective nor purely subjective. I find Kant's account more satisfying because it captures the normativity of aesthetic experience — the sense that good taste can be right and bad taste wrong — without claiming beauty is a property of objects like mass or charge.

commonMistakes: Simply asserting 'it's subjective' or 'it's objective' without philosophical argument
Not being aware that Hume and Kant both tried to stake out a middle ground
Conflating 'no universal agreement' with 'therefore pure subjectivism'
followUps: What does your view imply about whether we can meaningfully teach someone to appreciate art?

question: Some people say philosophy is impractical. How would you respond to that challenge?
lookingFor: A genuine defence of philosophy's practical relevance that goes beyond 'critical thinking skills' — specific examples of where philosophical reasoning has mattered.

bullets: Acknowledge what the objection captures — much philosophy is abstract and theoretical
Give specific examples where philosophical reasoning has had real practical consequences
Show the long game: foundations matter even when they don't appear in daily decisions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The charge has some bite — a lot of professional philosophy does operate at a very abstract level. But I think it confuses 'not immediately applicable' with 'impractical'. The debate over what counts as a person directly shapes abortion law, end-of-life care, and AI rights. Questions about distributive justice are foundational to welfare policy. And the statistical reasoning behind medical trials depends on philosophical choices about probability and causation. Beyond specific cases, I think the more interesting point is that everyone operates with implicit philosophical commitments — about what evidence is, what fairness demands, what counts as a good reason — and people who don't examine those commitments are more likely to reason badly, not less.

commonMistakes: Retreating to 'it teaches you to think' without concrete examples
Being defensive rather than genuinely engaging the objection
Overclaiming — suggesting philosophy directly runs the world
followUps: Is there a branch of philosophy you think genuinely is too abstract to matter? Why?

question: Do you think the existence of God is a question philosophy can make progress on? Why or why not?
lookingFor: Philosophical rather than theological engagement — awareness of the main arguments and counter-arguments, and a view on whether rational argument can settle religious questions.

bullets: Distinguish the philosophical question (can reason evaluate the existence of God?) from the personal question (do you believe?)
Engage at least one argument for or against and its standard objection
Take a view on whether philosophical argument can make genuine progress here
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think philosophy can make genuine progress even if it can't deliver a proof in either direction. The ontological argument is philosophically interesting regardless of whether you find it convincing — Kant's objection that existence isn't a predicate has implications well beyond theology. The problem of evil is the most powerful argument against a traditional theist God, and responses like the free-will defence are philosophically evaluable — we can ask whether free will of the relevant kind is possible, or whether it could justify the amount of suffering we observe. Where I think philosophy struggles is with the 'reformed epistemology' move — the idea that belief in God can be properly basic, not requiring argument — because it feels like it places religious belief beyond evaluation, which ends the philosophical conversation rather than extending it.

commonMistakes: Stating your personal belief and treating that as an answer to the philosophical question
Treating philosophy of religion as trying to settle whether God exists, rather than what rationality demands
Not knowing any of the classic arguments well enough to engage them
followUps: How would you respond to someone who said faith by definition doesn't require philosophical justification?

question: How do you respond when someone argues a position you've never considered and find it genuinely compelling — even if it contradicts something you already believed?
lookingFor: Intellectual honesty and genuine openness to revision — essential for philosophical study — without being a pushover who drops views at the first challenge.

bullets: Show genuine openness to revising beliefs under argument, not just politeness
Distinguish being convinced by argument from being pressured by social dynamics
Reflect on what good philosophical dialogue actually requires
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Honestly, it's one of the things I find most exciting about philosophy — the moment when an argument actually moves you. I try to distinguish between finding an argument I can't immediately answer and being convinced by it: sometimes you need time to figure out what's wrong with it. But if I sit with it and the counter-arguments I reach feel weaker, I try to update my view, even when that's uncomfortable. I had that experience reading Singer's arguments on global poverty — I couldn't dismiss the logic and that was genuinely disorienting. I think that disorientation is the point of philosophy, not a failure. What I try not to do is pretend to update just to seem open-minded — that's worse than stubbornness.

commonMistakes: Claiming to never change your mind as a sign of intellectual strength
Claiming to immediately accept any argument you find compelling — showing no critical resistance
Treating intellectual humility as the same as not having views
followUps: What's a view you've genuinely changed your mind on, and what changed it?
